serving.natural_language_portion: One standard mushroom burger weighing approximately 180 g, comprising a wheat bun, grilled portobello mushroom patty, lettuce, sliced tomato, sliced cheese, and mayonnaise.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom Burger — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Mushroom Burger (cr_id F05020068) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mushroom burger in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: white wheat bread burger bun (GI ~65–70) moderated by the presence of dietary fibre (2.75 g/100g), protein (4.5 g/100g), fat (8.68 g/100g), and grilled portobello mushroom, which collectively lower the effective glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 34.1) / 100 = 20.4, based on 180 g serving providing 34.1 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 735 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should avoid or strictly limit mushroom burgers due to very high sodium content (735 mg/100 g) contributed by the bun, processed cheese, and mayonnaise; if consumed, request no added salt, minimal mayonnaise, and omit processed cheese to partially reduce sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (18.95 g/100 g) with low sugar (2.4 g/100 g) and meaningful dietary fibre (2.75 g/100 g) supporting a moderate glycaemic response; estimated GI 60
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes may consume a mushroom burger in a single-burger portion (approx. 180 g), as the fibre, protein, and fat content moderate the glycaemic response; however, a wholegrain bun should be requested where available, and high-sugar condiments such as ketchup or sweet chilli sauce should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (735 mg/100 g), estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from processed bun and mayonnaise), and saturated fat from cheese and mayonnaise present combined cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit mushroom burger consumption due to high sodium, saturated fat from cheese and mayonnaise, and estimated trans fat from the processed bun; if consumed, request reduced-fat cheese, minimal mayonnaise, and no added salt to partially mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) and high sodium (735 mg/100 g) are pro-inflammatory; processed bun and mayonnaise contribute to overall inflammatory load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid mushroom burgers in standard preparation due to estimated trans fat and high sodium; the grilled portobello mushroom provides beta-glucan anti-inflammatory benefit but is outweighed by the pro-inflammatory profile of the processed components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A mushroom burger is a Western-style sandwich consisting of a wheat burger bun filled with a grilled portobello mushroom patty, fresh lettuce, sliced tomato, sliced processed cheese, and mayonnaise. The portobello mushroom serves as the primary protein and umami component in place of a meat patty, giving the burger a meaty, earthy texture and flavour. The bun is soft and slightly chewy, while the mayonnaise adds creaminess and the tomato and lettuce provide freshness and crunch. This dish is classified as vegetarian and is commonly served as a lunch or dinner option in Western-style cafes, fast-food outlets, and food courts in Singapore and globally. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 177 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 8.68 g fat, 18.95 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g sugar, 2.75 g dietary fibre, and 735 mg sodium. The high sodium content (735 mg/100 g) makes it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The moderate carbohydrate content with meaningful fibre makes it acceptable for type 2 diabetes management in controlled portions. The portobello mushroom contributes beta-glucan, iron (1.63 mg/100 g), and calcium (61 mg/100 g). The estimated glycaemic index is 60 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 20.4 per standard 180 g serving.
common_user_queries: Is mushroom burger suitable for diabetics?
Is mushroom burger high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mushroom Burger (cr_id F05020068) with a reported confidence of 0.95. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) but is documented with full reasoning.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this mushroom burger composite not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this composite dish. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on estimated contribution from processed bun and commercial mayonnaise consistent with the input health_suitability assessment; this is an estimate and not directly measured in the HPB source. saturated_fat_g estimated at 2.1 g/100 g based on the fat profile of cheese and mayonnaise components consistent with USDA FoodData Central analogous composite data; not directly reported in HPB source. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on the wheat bun GI moderated by fibre, protein, and fat content — no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (60 × 34.1) / 100 = 20.4 based on 180 g serving size providing 34.1 g carbohydrate. This is a Western-cuisine dish; multilingual naming requirements (Singaporean language matrix) do not apply. local_names fields are null as this is a Western dish without established local-language names in the Singaporean context.
